title: Praxis School Psychologist (5403) learning library
description: Twelve source-reviewed chapters aligned to the four ETS 5403 categories and ten NASP practice domains, with direct links to every diagnostic, simulation, and targeted-practice item.
reviewStandard: AlloFlow Praxis School Psychologist 5403 learning-library source and editorial review v1

simulation.note: Independent timed simulation using original AlloFlow items; not an official ETS form, scaled score, pass prediction, credential, psychological evaluation, diagnosis, or treatment tool.

legalCaution: This school-psychology content is educational preparation, not a psychological evaluation, diagnosis, mental-health treatment, or legal advice. Verify current federal, state, local, district, credential, ethical, crisis, consent, privacy, and mandated-reporting requirements for practice.
frameworkCaution: The ETS 5403 study companion defines the tested blueprint. NASP standards, ethics, and practice materials can be revised; verify current editions while preparing and practicing.

chapters.title: Problem Solving and Multimethod Data Integration
chapters.domain: Professional Practices That Permeate Service Delivery
chapters.summary: Use a RIOT-informed problem-solving cycle to define concerns, analyze alterable causes, select interventions, monitor response and fidelity, and communicate integrated conclusions.
objectives: Operationalize referral concerns and select relevant sources.
Link hypotheses to interventions and progress measures.
Integrate evidence into actionable reports and decisions.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: From referral label to measurable problem
sections.content: Begin by replacing labels such as unmotivated, defiant, or low ability with an observable discrepancy between current and expected performance. Specify behavior or skill, setting, task, frequency or accuracy, duration, relevant comparison, baseline, and student-valued outcome. Review developmental, instructional, attendance, language, health, sensory, intervention, and family history only because it can clarify the question. A precise definition prevents assessment from becoming a routine battery searching for a problem.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: RIOT and ecological hypothesis testing
sections.content: RIOT organizes records, interviews, observations, and tests or other direct measures. Select sources that can confirm the concern, reveal contexts and exceptions, identify strengths, and distinguish skill, performance, instructional, language, health, and environmental contributors. Disagreement across sources is information: compare setting, task, informant, timing, opportunity, and measure quality. Form testable hypotheses about alterable variables instead of treating a disability label or trait judgment as an explanation.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Intervention, monitoring, and fidelity
sections.content: Match intervention mechanism to the hypothesis. Missing skills call for explicit instruction and practice; inconsistent performance may require antecedent changes, efficient prompts, motivation, or reinforcement; escape-maintained behavior requires task and replacement-skill changes. Use a sensitive, repeatable aligned measure, a goal line, and a prespecified decision rule. Collect adherence, dosage, quality, and access data so weak outcomes can be distinguished from incomplete implementation.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Integration and communication
sections.content: Integrate patterns rather than listing sources in isolation. Answer each referral question, describe strengths and needs, explain convergence and discrepancy, state uncertainty and limitations, and connect findings to feasible supports and outcome measures. Reports and meetings should use accessible language, labeled visuals, respectful person-centered descriptions, and opportunities for student and family questions. A score is useful only through a defensible interpretation tied to educational action.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A teacher says a student is “unmotivated.” What should the school psychologist do first?
choices: Define the concern in observable, measurable terms and identify when, where, and under what conditions it occurs.
Select an intelligence test immediately.
Accept the label as the problem definition.
Recommend special education before gathering data.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: A useful problem definition names an observable performance, context, baseline, and desired change so assessment and intervention can be aligned.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which assessment plan best represents RIOT?
choices: Administer one norm-referenced test and stop.
Review records, interview relevant people, observe directly, and test or otherwise measure the target as appropriate.
Rely only on a teacher rating scale.
Collect records without observing current performance.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: RIOT organizes record review, interviews, observations, and tests; sources should be selected because they answer the referral question.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A newly enrolled student is referred for reading difficulty. Which background information is most useful before selecting measures?
choices: Only the student’s current grade.
The disability category of a sibling.
Instructional history, language exposure, attendance, prior interventions and response, health or sensory information, and developmental and family context.
A national reading average without local opportunity data.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Background evidence helps distinguish skill need, access, opportunity, language, health, and contextual explanations and guides efficient assessment.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A universal screener identifies a student as at risk. What is the most defensible interpretation?
choices: The student has a confirmed disability.
The score must be ignored because screeners are brief.
The student should be retained automatically.
The result signals need for follow-up and support; it is not by itself a diagnosis or special-education eligibility decision.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Screening prioritizes further assessment or prevention; classification requires additional, appropriate evidence and lawful team procedures.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: After defining a math-fluency problem, what should the team do next in a problem-solving model?
choices: Analyze likely skill, performance, instructional, and contextual contributors and develop testable hypotheses.
Choose the most familiar intervention without analysis.
Wait for the problem to become severe.
Assign a fixed trait label as the cause.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Problem analysis links plausible, alterable causes to evidence and produces hypotheses that can guide intervention selection.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Measurement and Assessment of Diverse Learners
chapters.domain: Professional Practices That Permeate Service Delivery
chapters.summary: Interpret reliability, validity, score uncertainty, norms, and multiple assessment methods while preserving construct access and nondiscriminatory practice.
objectives: Explain score precision and validity for a specific use.
Select academic, cognitive, behavioral, adaptive, and developmental methods appropriately.
Plan culturally, linguistically, and disability-responsive assessment.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Reliability, error, and score precision
sections.content: Reliability concerns consistency or precision under relevant conditions. Standard error of measurement and confidence intervals express expected imprecision around an obtained score; they discourage rank-ordering students on trivial differences. Reliability can vary by score, population, task, and purpose. Interrater agreement matters for observations and ratings, internal consistency for item sets, and alternate-form or test-retest evidence for repeated decisions. No reliability coefficient makes a measure valid for every inference.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Validity, constructs, and norms
sections.content: Validity is the evidence and reasoning supporting a specific interpretation and use for a population and context. Ask what construct is intended, what irrelevant skills may affect performance, whether content and response processes fit, how scores relate to relevant outcomes, and what consequences follow. Percentile ranks show relative standing in a named norm group and are not percent correct. Standard scores, age or grade norms, and classifications require relevant contemporary comparisons and honest uncertainty.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: A purpose-driven assessment toolbox
sections.content: Cognitive, achievement, processing, curriculum-based, performance, rating, interview, observation, self-report, adaptive, and developmental methods answer different questions. Cognitive tests do not become automatic gates to services; CBM is well suited to repeated academic monitoring; self-report can reveal private experience but needs developmental and response-style review; adaptive ratings describe typical everyday functioning across contexts. Select the least burdensome sources capable of answering the decision and integrate them.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Language, culture, disability, and access
sections.content: Distinguish difference from disability by examining language proficiency across languages, instruction and opportunity, mobility, acculturation, family evidence, and intervention response. Use qualified interpreters and measures or procedures in the form most likely to yield accurate information. For sensory, motor, communication, or health needs, remove construct-irrelevant barriers while preserving the target; document accommodations, departures from standardization, and limits on score interpretation.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A test produces highly inconsistent scores under similar conditions. Which property is weak?
choices: Reliability, because the scores lack sufficient consistency or precision for the intended decision.
Face validity, because the test may look unfamiliar.
Percentile rank, because norms are unnecessary.
Fidelity, because the intervention was not measured.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Reliability concerns consistency and measurement precision; unreliable scores limit the defensibility of subsequent interpretations.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What does a standard error of measurement communicate?
choices: The percentage of items answered incorrectly.
Expected score imprecision, supporting a range of plausible values rather than false precision from one obtained score.
Proof that the test is culturally fair.
The student’s exact future score.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: SEM and confidence intervals reflect measurement error and discourage treating an obtained score as a perfectly precise trait value.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A reliable vocabulary test is used to infer fine-motor ability. What is the problem?
choices: Reliability guarantees all interpretations.
The score should be converted to a percentile.
Validity evidence does not support that interpretation and use for the different construct.
Norms eliminate construct mismatch.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Validity concerns evidence for a specific interpretation, purpose, population, and context; it is not guaranteed by consistency.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student earns the 70th percentile on a norm-referenced measure. What does this mean?
choices: The student answered exactly that percentage correctly.
The student mastered that percentage of the curriculum.
The score is a diagnostic probability.
The score is at or above the performance of that percentage of the relevant norm group, not a percent-correct score.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Percentile rank describes relative standing in a specified norm group and should be explained with norm relevance and uncertainty.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is the most appropriate role of a cognitive measure in an evaluation?
choices: Use it only when it answers a referral question, interpret patterns cautiously, and integrate it with achievement, context, observation, and other evidence.
Use the full-scale score as the sole eligibility criterion.
Treat cognitive scores as fixed and culture free.
Administer it automatically in every referral.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Cognitive assessment is one possible source; IDEA requires varied tools and prohibits a single measure as the sole criterion.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Consultation and Collaborative Problem Solving
chapters.domain: Professional Practices That Permeate Service Delivery
chapters.summary: Build shared ownership through clear goals, respectful communication, feasible plans, role clarity, language access, and outcome-focused follow-up.
objectives: Apply a structured consultation cycle.
Resolve disagreement through evidence and common goals.
Coordinate families, educators, and community partners within privacy boundaries.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: A consultation cycle
sections.content: Establish a collaborative relationship, clarify roles, operationalize the concern, review context and strengths, set a shared measurable goal, analyze causes, select a feasible evidence-informed plan, define responsibilities, implement with support, and review fidelity and outcomes. Consultation develops another person’s capacity rather than transferring the classroom, family, or service role to the psychologist. The meeting itself is participation, not evidence that the concern improved.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Feasibility, acceptability, and implementation
sections.content: A theoretically strong intervention can fail when it does not fit routines, resources, culture, skill, or workload. Ask what will make the plan usable, distinguish core mechanism from adaptable features, model and rehearse procedures, provide materials and feedback, and create a light fidelity check. If a consultee hesitates, explore the barrier rather than labeling resistance. Adapt collaboratively and document which version is being tested.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Communication across difference and disagreement
sections.content: Use plain language, visuals, teach-back, active listening, and interpreters or translated material as needed. Treat conflicting home and school reports as ecological evidence rather than selecting a winner. During team conflict, return to the student-valued outcome, make assumptions explicit, identify common evidence and uncertainty, compare harms and feasibility, and select a reversible test with a review date. Power and professional jargon should not substitute for reasoning.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Community coordination and consultation outcomes
sections.content: Outside providers can contribute when purpose, consent, roles, secure information sharing, school responsibilities, and follow-up are clear. Share only authorized necessary information. Evaluate consultation through implementation, student outcomes, feasibility, satisfaction, capacity growth, and unintended effects. A supported referral addresses fit, cost, transportation, language, availability, and continuity instead of ending school support after a phone number is given.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is the first task in behavioral consultation?
choices: Build a collaborative relationship and define the concern, context, baseline, and desired change in observable terms.
Prescribe a plan before hearing the teacher’s perspective.
Assume the student is the sole cause.
Take permanent control of the classroom.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Consultation begins with rapport and shared problem identification so the plan is relevant, feasible, and measurable.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A teacher agrees with a plan but doubts it can fit the class routine. What is the best response?
choices: Insist on the original plan without discussion.
Explore the barrier, preserve essential components, simplify delivery collaboratively, and define supports and fidelity checks.
Document noncompliance and end consultation.
Remove all measurement requirements.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Feasibility and acceptability affect implementation; collaborative adaptation should retain the mechanism while responding to context.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A consultation goal is “the student will behave better.” How should it be revised?
choices: Keep it broad so any change counts.
Choose a goal without the student or family.
Specify the observable behavior, condition, baseline, achievable target, measurement method, and review date.
Use a diagnostic label as the goal.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Operational goals permit shared implementation and evaluation and should be meaningful to the student and stakeholders.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A parent and teacher offer different explanations for a student’s avoidance. What should the psychologist do?
choices: Declare the school perspective more objective.
Average the explanations without analysis.
Exclude the student from further discussion.
Validate both perspectives, compare contexts and evidence, identify common goals, and test a hypothesis rather than choosing sides.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Collaborative problem solving uses discrepancies as ecological information and builds a shared, testable understanding.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A meeting becomes dominated by technical assessment language. How can the psychologist improve collaboration?
choices: Pause, use plain language and accessible visuals, check understanding, invite questions, and connect evidence to decisions.
Continue to preserve the agenda.
Ask the family to read the report later.
Use more acronyms to demonstrate precision.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Effective communication is reciprocal, accessible, culturally responsive, and tied to meaningful choices rather than professional display.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Academic Interventions and Instructional Supports
chapters.summary: Match explicit and differentiated instruction, access supports, self-regulation, and progress monitoring to specific academic skills and contextual barriers.
objectives: Design explicit instruction from error analysis.
Distinguish accommodation from modification.
Set measurable goals and promote self-regulated learning.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Instruction follows error analysis
sections.content: An overall low score does not specify whether the learner lacks a concept, strategy, prerequisite, fluency, language access, or sufficient practice. Examine correct and incorrect responses, task conditions, instructional history, and opportunity. Explicit instruction models the target and thinking, prompts many accurate responses, gives immediate specific feedback, checks understanding, uses cumulative review, and releases responsibility when data show readiness rather than after a fixed number of lessons.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Differentiation, accommodation, and modification
sections.content: Differentiation varies examples, scaffolds, grouping, pacing, representation, and response options while pursuing a coherent core goal. An accommodation removes a barrier unrelated to the target without changing what is taught or measured. A modification changes content, complexity, or expected performance and must be decided and documented appropriately. Assistive technology should be selected through task-barrier analysis, authentic trials, training, usability, independence, and outcome evidence.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Goals and self-regulation
sections.content: A measurable academic goal names conditions, observable performance, baseline, criterion or magnitude, timeframe, and measurement method. Student ownership grows when learners set proximal goals, select and use strategies, self-monitor accurately, compare results with criteria, and adjust. Model each routine, provide feedback, and fade adult prompts deliberately. Independence is an outcome to measure; permanent close prompting can hide skill and create dependence.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Evidence-based selection and progress decisions
sections.content: Integrate credible intervention research with the specific skill, learner characteristics, language and culture, setting, resources, and implementation capacity. Monitor using repeatable measures sensitive to the target and graph level, trend, variability, and goal. Apply an advance decision rule only after checking fidelity and access. Continue when response is adequate, adapt the mechanism when error patterns change, intensify when growth is insufficient, and reconsider the hypothesis when implementation was strong but outcomes remain weak.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student has not mastered a multi-step computation procedure. Which instruction is strongest?
choices: Explicit modeling, guided practice with many responses, immediate feedback, cumulative review, and gradual release based on data.
Independent worksheets without instruction.
A reward for correct answers without teaching.
A lower-grade placement based on one error sample.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Explicit instruction makes the strategy visible and shifts responsibility only when evidence shows readiness.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Students in one class vary widely in background knowledge. What is appropriate differentiation?
choices: Assign unrelated easier goals to any struggling student.
Preserve the core goal while adjusting examples, grouping, scaffolds, pacing, and response options based on need.
Use identical delivery because fairness requires sameness.
Remove students needing support from core instruction.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Differentiation varies access and support while maintaining meaningful expectations and monitoring learning.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes an accommodation from a modification?
choices: Both terms mean any helpful strategy.
An accommodation always lowers standards.
An accommodation removes an irrelevant barrier while preserving the target; a modification changes content, complexity, or expected performance.
A modification can never be documented in an IEP.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: The key distinction is whether the learning or measurement target remains the same. This answer applies the complete school-psychology principle while preserving assessment, access, safety, and decision quality.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which instructional goal is most measurable?
choices: The student will try harder in reading.
The teacher will provide ten lessons.
Reading will become age appropriate soon.
Given comparable grade-level passages, the student will increase an identified reading measure from baseline to a defined criterion by a stated date.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Measurable goals include condition, observable behavior, baseline, criterion, timeframe, and measurement method.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: How can a psychologist promote student self-regulation?
choices: Teach goal setting, self-monitoring, strategy selection, and self-evaluation, then fade prompts as accurate use grows.
Keep continuous adult prompts indefinitely.
Punish every missed step.
Remove all challenging work.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Self-regulated learning requires explicit strategy instruction, feedback, practice, and planned transfer of control.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Mental and Behavioral Health Services
chapters.summary: Provide tiered prevention and developmentally responsive supports using risk and protective factors, trauma-informed practice, brief counseling, and outcome evidence.
objectives: Differentiate primary, secondary, and tertiary supports.
Apply trauma-informed and theory-linked interventions.
Recognize concern patterns without diagnosing from signs alone.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: A continuum from promotion to intensive care
sections.content: Primary or Tier 1 services build protective skills and safe environments for broad populations. Secondary or Tier 2 supports target students with shared elevated need. Tertiary or Tier 3 services are intensive and individualized and may include coordinated community treatment. Tiers describe support, not identity. Use screening and referral only when follow-up capacity, consent or notice requirements, culturally responsive procedures, and clear crisis pathways are in place.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Risk, protection, and trauma-informed care
sections.content: Assess biological, developmental, family, peer, school, community, cultural, and historical influences without treating risk as destiny. Protective relationships, belonging, coping, problem solving, safe predictable routines, identity affirmation, and service access can be strengthened. Trauma-informed practice emphasizes physical and emotional safety, choice, voice, regulation, connection, and avoidance of coercive disclosure or deficit assumptions; it is a service framework, not a diagnosis.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Counseling theories as mechanisms
sections.content: Person-centered conditions include empathy, genuineness, and nonjudgmental regard. CBT connects thoughts, feelings, physiology, and behavior through reappraisal and practice. Solution-focused work identifies preferred outcomes, exceptions, strengths, scaling, and small next steps. Applied behavioral methods change antecedents, teach skills, and arrange consequences based on function. Adapt language, activity, autonomy, and family involvement to development and culture while preserving safety and the agreed goal.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Concern recognition, referral, and evaluation
sections.content: Withdrawal, irritability, loss of interest, worry, somatic complaints, inattention, aggression, or mood change may signal several mental-health, medical, developmental, contextual, or environmental concerns. Assess safety and educational functioning, collect multi-source evidence, avoid diagnosing from a checklist, and connect the student with qualified evaluation or care. Evaluate school interventions with baseline, repeated outcomes, fidelity, participation, student experience, equity, adverse effects, generalization, and maintenance.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which service is a primary prevention example?
choices: Teach a universal evidence-informed coping and help-seeking curriculum and monitor schoolwide reach and outcomes.
Provide individual therapy only after crisis.
Create an intensive plan for one student.
Wait for diagnoses before offering support.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Primary prevention is broadly available and aims to reduce risk or build protective skills before severe concerns develop.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A classroom has frequent transitions and unclear expectations. What is the best initial behavioral support?
choices: Remove recess from the entire class.
Clarify and teach routines, improve prompts and pacing, increase active engagement, and reinforce expected behavior consistently.
Refer every student for evaluation.
Ignore the environment and counsel one student.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Proactive classroom management changes predictable antecedents, teaches expectations, and reinforces engagement.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which set best illustrates protective factors?
choices: Exposure to violence and family conflict.
Chronic absence and social isolation.
Supportive adult relationships, belonging, coping and problem-solving skills, safe routines, and access to effective services.
Repeated academic failure without support.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Protective factors reduce risk impact and can be strengthened at student, family, school, and community levels.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student becomes hypervigilant after a frightening event. What is trauma-informed support?
choices: Require a detailed public retelling.
Treat avoidance as deliberate defiance.
Suspend all expectations indefinitely.
Increase safety, predictability, choice, regulation, and connection; avoid forced disclosure and coordinate additional support as needed.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Trauma-informed practice supports regulation and agency without coercion, blame, or assumptions about diagnosis.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student says, “One mistake means I am a total failure.” Which approach fits CBT?
choices: Identify the thought, examine evidence and effects, develop a balanced alternative, and practice a useful behavior.
Treat the belief as factual.
Focus only on rewards without cognition.
Avoid all situations that evoke discomfort.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: CBT links cognitions, emotion, physiology, and behavior and uses collaborative reappraisal and practice.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Behavior Analysis, Counseling, and Student Crisis Response
chapters.summary: Use observable definitions and functional assessment to design replacement-skill plans, deliver active counseling supports, and respond immediately to student safety concerns.
objectives: Conduct a functional behavior assessment.
Design prevention, teaching, and reinforcement around function.
Apply safety-first crisis and supported-referral procedures.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Operational definition and ABC evidence
sections.content: Define behavior through visible actions with examples, nonexamples, and a unit such as frequency, rate, duration, latency, or interval. Gather antecedent-behavior-consequence information across occurrences and nonoccurrences, settings, tasks, people, and time. Interviews and rating scales generate hypotheses; direct observation tests them. A disability label, emotion, or moral judgment is not a function. Common consequences may involve attention, escape, tangibles, or automatic effects, but function must be demonstrated.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Function-matched intervention
sections.content: A useful plan prevents predictable triggers, changes instruction or environment, teaches an efficient acceptable response serving the same function, reinforces that response, avoids inadvertently strengthening problem behavior, and monitors outcomes and fidelity. Escape-maintained disruption may require task adjustment and help or break requests; attention-maintained behavior may require planned attention for an appropriate bid. Punishment alone does not teach the needed skill or correct the setting event.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Counseling groups and generalization
sections.content: Screen students for shared need, readiness, safety, developmental fit, and whether another service is needed. Explain purpose, participation conditions, records, counselor privacy limits, and inability to guarantee peer confidentiality. Teach skills explicitly with modeling, rehearsal, feedback, and practice in real settings. Coordinate with educators and families as authorized, collect student-centered outcomes, and plan maintenance and generalization rather than assuming group-room performance will transfer.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Immediate risk and continuity
sections.content: For a specific imminent suicide, self-harm, or violence concern, maintain appropriate supervision, activate the current school crisis and threat procedures, involve required caregivers or emergency supports, reduce access to means when within role, document, and ensure follow-up. Never promise secrecy. When need exceeds school scope or competence, make a supported referral that addresses consent, resource fit, transportation, language, cost, connection, and continued school-based support.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which definition of aggression is most useful for observation?
choices: A description of visible actions with clear examples and nonexamples that independent observers can identify.
Behavior that is bad or disrespectful.
Any action caused by anger.
Whatever the teacher dislikes.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Operational definitions improve reliability, baseline measurement, hypothesis testing, and intervention evaluation.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student calls out mainly when independent work begins and teacher help follows. What should an FBA examine?
choices: Assume attention is always the function.
Compare antecedents and consequences across occurrences and nonoccurrences to test whether help or task escape maintains behavior.
Use the disability label as the functional analysis.
Select punishment before collecting data.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: FBA tests relations among context, antecedents, observable behavior, and consequences rather than guessing intent.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: An FBA suggests escape from difficult work. Which replacement behavior is best?
choices: A generic rule to be good.
A behavior unrelated to escape or support.
An efficient, acceptable request for help or a brief break while instruction and task difficulty are also adjusted.
Silence without access to assistance.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: A replacement response should serve the same function efficiently while prevention, instruction, and reinforcement change the broader context.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is positive reinforcement?
choices: Anything the adult intends as a reward.
Removing an unpleasant stimulus regardless of effect.
Adding a penalty to reduce behavior.
A consequence is presented after behavior and the future frequency of that behavior increases.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Reinforcement is defined by an increase in future behavior, not by the provider’s intention or the pleasantness of the event.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which small-group plan is most appropriate for social problem solving?
choices: Screening for fit, explicit modeling, rehearsal, feedback, real-setting practice, progress data, and privacy expectations.
Unstructured disclosure without a skill target.
One lecture with no rehearsal.
Placement based only on schedule convenience.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Effective skill groups select compatible needs and provide active teaching, generalization, and outcome monitoring.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Schoolwide Learning, MTSS, and Systems Change
chapters.summary: Use needs assessment, integrated MTSS, equitable screening, implementation supports, and program evaluation to improve learning and behavior at scale.
objectives: Build system priorities from disaggregated need and resources.
Evaluate screening and policy decisions.
Support implementation, equity, and scale.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Needs, assets, and resource mapping
sections.content: Define a system concern with disaggregated achievement, attendance, behavior, climate, screening, and access data. Add student, family, staff, and community perspectives and map existing programs, personnel, schedules, partnerships, funding, and gaps. A needs assessment should identify who experiences the problem, where and when, likely contributors, current strengths, and the capacity to respond. Purchasing a popular program before this work risks solving the wrong problem.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Integrated MTSS
sections.content: An effective MTSS aligns academic, behavioral, and social-emotional promotion, screening, tiered evidence-informed support, progress monitoring, fidelity, data teams, and problem solving. Universal services continue while students receive targeted or intensive help; tiers are not placements or eligibility categories. Decision rules should trigger timely support, not waiting for severe failure, and MTSS must not delay an evaluation required under law. Integration reduces duplicate teams and conflicting plans.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Screening and policy evaluation
sections.content: A screener needs evidence for the intended use, representative participation, equitable access, clear decision rules, manageable false-positive and false-negative consequences, and follow-up capacity. Evaluate policies such as retention, tracking, discipline, testing, and promotion using credible research and local subgroup outcomes, opportunity, burden, and unintended effects. Tradition and schoolwide averages do not establish effectiveness or fairness.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Implementation, scale, and equity
sections.content: Identify core components and adaptable features, then provide leadership, training, coaching, materials, protected time, data systems, and rapid feedback. Monitor adherence, dosage, quality, reach, responsiveness, adaptations, burden, and outcomes. When aggregate results improve but a subgroup gap grows, examine referral and decision points, access, definitions, bias, and affected voices. Scale only after demonstrating feasible implementation and meaningful equitable benefit.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A district is selecting a schoolwide priority. What should occur first?
choices: Conduct a needs assessment using disaggregated outcomes, screening, stakeholder voice, resource mapping, and current-practice evidence.
Choose the most marketed program.
Rely on one administrator’s impression.
Wait until every student is failing.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Systems planning begins with a defined need, existing assets, access patterns, stakeholder evidence, and implementation context.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What makes MTSS an integrated framework?
choices: Assign each student a permanent tier label.
Align universal, targeted, and intensive supports through common problem solving, screening, progress data, fidelity, and coordinated teams.
Use tiers only for special-education eligibility.
Eliminate individualized supports.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: MTSS integrates prevention and increasing intensity across domains through data and coordinated implementation.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: How should a universal screener be evaluated at the system level?
choices: The number of colorful reports produced.
Whether every score is normally distributed.
Technical adequacy, participation, subgroup fairness, decision rules, follow-up capacity, false positives and negatives, and outcomes.
Only the vendor’s national claims.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Screening is useful only when scores support equitable decisions and the system can respond appropriately to identified needs.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A retention policy is associated with poor outcomes for several groups. What should the psychologist recommend?
choices: Preserve the policy because it is traditional.
End it immediately without implementation planning.
Report only the schoolwide average.
Review credible research and local disaggregated evidence, engage affected stakeholders, test alternatives, and monitor intended and unintended effects.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Systems decisions integrate evidence, equity, stakeholder context, feasibility, and evaluation rather than tradition or abrupt unsupported change.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A schoolwide intervention works in the pilot but not after expansion. What should be examined?
choices: Whether core components, training, coaching, leadership, resources, fidelity, and contextual adaptations were supported at scale.
Whether students became less motivated everywhere.
Whether the program logo changed.
Whether all local adaptation should be prohibited.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Implementation systems determine whether an evidence-based practice can be delivered with quality and fit across settings.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Safe and Supportive Schools and Crisis Systems
chapters.summary: Build preventive school-climate, behavior, bullying, threat, suicide, and crisis systems that are coordinated, equitable, accessible, and continuously evaluated.
objectives: Assess climate and implement preventive behavior supports.
Distinguish bullying from balanced conflict.
Design threat, suicide, crisis, and recovery systems.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Climate and universal prevention
sections.content: School climate includes safety, belonging, relationships, teaching and learning conditions, fairness, physical environment, and participation. Combine confidential perception data from students, families, and staff with attendance, discipline, achievement, referral, observation, and qualitative evidence. Universal positive behavior support defines and teaches expectations, improves routines, acknowledges appropriate behavior, responds consistently and instructionally, and uses disaggregated data. Lack of reports is not proof of safety.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Bullying and violence prevention
sections.content: Bullying involves repeated or likely repeated aggression and a power imbalance, so peer mediation and equal compromise may endanger or blame the targeted student. Use immediate protection, authorized investigation, accountability, support, family communication, climate and bystander work, and monitoring under current policy. Violence prevention also strengthens connection, reporting pathways, conflict skills, supportive adults, equitable discipline, and timely access to mental-health services.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Threat and suicide assessment systems
sections.content: Behavioral threat assessment uses a trained multidisciplinary process to investigate communication, context, intent, planning, capability, stressors, targets, access, protective factors, and supports; it does not profile or predict from one score. Suicide systems combine promotion, prevention, gatekeeper training, identification, safe referral, trained risk assessment, caregiver and community coordination, means safety within role, emergency procedures, postvention, and outcome review. Screening without response capacity is unsafe.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Crisis preparedness, response, and recovery
sections.content: A comprehensive plan covers prevention and mitigation, incident roles, communication, physical safety, accessibility, family reunification, psychological triage, continuity, responder support, training and exercises, recovery, and after-action improvement. During an event, use verified information and assigned command roles. Recovery continues over time and monitors functioning, access, new risk, service use, equity, family and staff feedback, implementation, and the need to revise supports and plans.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: How should school climate be assessed?
choices: Combine anonymous stakeholder surveys, attendance and discipline patterns, observations, focus groups, referral data, and responsible subgroup analysis.
Use one administrator rating.
Count cameras as the climate outcome.
Treat absence of reports as proof of safety.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Climate is multidimensional and requires converging perception, participation, behavioral, and outcome evidence.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is a core feature of schoolwide positive behavioral support?
choices: Rely on exclusion after problems occur.
Define and teach a small set of expectations, acknowledge them, respond instructionally and consistently, and use data for improvement.
Assume expectations are already understood.
Use one consequence for every function and context.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Schoolwide support explicitly teaches behavior, improves environments, reinforces expectations, and uses data to refine implementation.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Why is peer mediation often inappropriate for bullying?
choices: Require equal compromise from both sides.
Ask the targeted student to ignore it.
The power imbalance undermines safe voluntary negotiation; use protection, authorized investigation, accountability, and systemic prevention.
Keep the report confidential from required responders.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Bullying differs from balanced conflict and requires safety, policy-based response, and attention to power.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is the purpose of behavioral threat assessment?
choices: Automatically expel anyone who uses threatening words.
Use a checklist score as the sole decision.
Assume no risk if the student denies intent.
Use a trained multidisciplinary process to investigate context, intent, capability, stressors, protective factors, and needed supports rather than profile or predict from one trait.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Threat assessment is a structured, context-based prevention process focused on facts, supports, management, and ongoing monitoring.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What belongs in a comprehensive suicide-prevention system?
choices: Promotion and prevention, staff and student education, identification and referral pathways, trained risk response, caregiver and community coordination, postvention, and evaluation.
Only an annual assembly.
Screening without follow-up capacity.
A promise that every disclosure remains secret.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: A comprehensive system connects prevention, identification, immediate response, continuity, postvention, training, and evaluation.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Family, School, and Community Collaboration
chapters.summary: Create reciprocal, culturally responsive family partnerships and coordinated community services that center strengths, access, consent, student voice, and shared outcomes.
objectives: Apply family-systems and strengths perspectives.
Remove barriers to meaningful family participation.
Coordinate home, wraparound, and transition supports.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Family systems and reciprocal expertise
sections.content: Families contribute history, culture, language, routines, relationships, priorities, strengths, and evidence outside school. Family-systems thinking examines reciprocal roles, boundaries, communication, stress, resources, and transitions rather than assigning the child or caregiver sole blame. Ask what the family wants to change, what already works, and what burden a plan creates. Participation means family evidence can influence the decision, not merely that a caregiver attended a meeting.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Access and trustworthy engagement
sections.content: Low participation may reflect work schedules, transportation, disability access, childcare, language, prior harm, technology, immigration concerns, or meetings that offer no meaningful role. Ask families directly about preferred communication, time, format, and contribution; vary options; provide qualified interpretation and translated material; and report how input shaped action. Do not use students as interpreters for sensitive decisions or treat one liaison as representative of every family.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Home intervention that fits
sections.content: Co-design a small strategy embedded in an authentic routine. Define the target and family-valued goal, model the procedure, identify feasible prompts and reinforcement, minimize materials and time, agree on simple outcome evidence, and schedule review. Preserve cultural values and caregiver authority. A complex school protocol copied into the home may be technically sound but unusable; acceptability, burden, and consistency are part of intervention quality.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Wraparound and transition coordination
sections.content: Complex needs may require a family-driven team with shared strengths-based outcomes, student voice, defined agency and school roles, consent and information boundaries, coordinated actions, crisis and continuity plans, and regular review. For postsecondary transition, begin early, center self-determination and measurable goals, invite agencies with required consent, address eligibility and access, and confirm connections. Another agency’s involvement does not automatically end school responsibilities.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What does a family-systems perspective add to intervention planning?
choices: Behavior is understood within reciprocal relationships, strengths, culture, stressors, boundaries, and transitions rather than blaming one member.
The child is always the sole cause.
Family patterns are fixed and cannot change.
School evidence makes family information unnecessary.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Family-systems thinking identifies interactions and supports that can be changed while respecting strengths and culture.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A school has low family participation in meetings scheduled during work hours. What is the best response?
choices: Conclude families do not value education.
Ask families about barriers and preferred roles, vary time and format, provide language and access supports, and share decision-making power.
Require attendance before services are offered.
Send more technical notices in the same format.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Authentic engagement removes access barriers and makes participation relevant, reciprocal, and influential.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which interpretation practice is most appropriate?
choices: Use a child as interpreter.
Proceed without interpretation to avoid delay.
Use a qualified interpreter, protect confidentiality, provide translated materials when possible, and communicate directly with the family.
Ask an untrained neighbor to interpret private data.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Qualified interpretation protects accuracy, privacy, dignity, and meaningful participation. This answer applies the complete school-psychology principle while preserving assessment, access, safety, and decision quality.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A family wants a home strategy for homework conflict. What is the best plan?
choices: Prescribe a complex plan without family input.
Assume school procedures transfer unchanged to home.
Focus on parent blame rather than alterable routines.
Co-design a simple culturally acceptable routine, teach and model it, define feasible reinforcement and data, and review burden and outcomes.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Home interventions should fit family priorities, resources, culture, and routines and be evaluated collaboratively.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What characterizes wraparound or interagency collaboration?
choices: A family-driven team develops shared strengths-based goals, defined roles, coordinated services, consent boundaries, and regular outcome review.
Each agency works independently.
The school transfers all obligations to one provider.
All information is shared without authorization.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Wraparound coordination is individualized, strengths based, family centered, team driven, privacy aware, and outcome focused.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Equitable and Culturally Responsive Practice
chapters.domain: Foundations of School Psychological Service Delivery
chapters.summary: Examine identity, language, disability, opportunity, bias, power, access, and system decision points to promote fair assessment, intervention, and outcomes.
objectives: Integrate culture without stereotyping.
Identify and interrupt implicit and systemic bias.
Evaluate disproportionality, access, and resource equity.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Culture and identity are evidence, not destiny
sections.content: Age, development, race, ethnicity, language, nationality, religion, gender identity, sexual orientation, disability, health, socioeconomic conditions, migration, and community context can shape experience and access. Ask how these factors matter for this student rather than applying group averages as traits. Use cultural humility: reflect on one’s limits, invite correction, seek community knowledge, and remain accountable. Culture-blind practice can hide construct barriers and unequal opportunity.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Bias at individual and system levels
sections.content: Implicit and explicit bias can affect which behavior is noticed, how it is described, who is referred, which tests are selected, how ambiguity is interpreted, and what placement follows. Operationalize criteria, use multiple sources, check decision consistency, examine disaggregated rates at each step, involve affected communities, and create accountability. Good intentions do not substitute for process evidence and outcome monitoring.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Language, disability, and construct access
sections.content: For multilingual learners, examine proficiency in relevant languages, instruction and opportunity, mobility, family history, culturally responsive intervention response, and appropriate assessment form. Qualified interpreters need briefing, role clarity, and debriefing. For disability-related sensory, motor, communication, or health barriers, preserve the intended construct while changing irrelevant response demands and documenting standardization and interpretive limits.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Disproportionality and equitable systems
sections.content: Analyze disproportionality across referral, screening, discipline, evaluation, eligibility, placement, course access, and service intensity rather than only final counts. A disparity is a signal for inquiry, not proof about each case. Correct system barriers without withholding individualized evaluation or support. Equity allocates responsive resources to address need and access barriers; evaluate reach, opportunity cost, burden, benefit, and unintended effects, not just equal invitation or distribution.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: How should culture enter assessment and intervention?
choices: Examine language, identity, values, opportunity, power, context, and within-group variation while avoiding stereotypes and preserving individual evidence.
Ignore culture in all decisions.
Assume group membership predicts behavior.
Use one cultural norm as universal.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Culturally responsive practice treats context as relevant evidence without reducing an individual to group assumptions.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A psychologist notices a stronger referral reaction to behavior from one racial group. What should happen?
choices: Rely on good intentions as proof of fairness.
Pause, operationalize the behavior, review disaggregated decision data, seek perspective and consultation, and change biased procedures.
Stop collecting demographic data.
Treat every referral as equally objective.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Bias mitigation requires reflective and systemic action, clear definitions, data, affected voices, and accountability.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What does social justice require in school psychology?
choices: Provide identical resources regardless of need.
Counsel individuals to tolerate the barrier.
Identify and remove systemic barriers, advocate with affected communities, and monitor whether resources and outcomes become more equitable.
Avoid policy issues as outside practice.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Equity and social justice address structures, access, power, and outcomes, not just equal wording or individual coping.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A multilingual learner struggles academically. Which evidence is essential?
choices: English test scores alone.
A nonverbal score as proof of no language influence.
The teacher’s diagnosis.
Language proficiency in relevant languages, quality and duration of instruction, opportunity, mobility, cultural context, intervention response, and appropriate assessment.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Difference from monolingual norms may reflect language and opportunity; comprehensive evidence is required.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A student with a sensory disability is evaluated. What is the central fairness question?
choices: Determine how to provide access so results reflect the intended construct, document deviations, and interpret limitations honestly.
Use standard administration even if it measures the disability.
Exclude the student from assessment.
Change scores to match peers.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Fair assessment minimizes construct-irrelevant barriers while maintaining transparency about standardization and inference.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Research, Evidence-Based Practice, and Program Evaluation
chapters.domain: Foundations of School Psychological Service Delivery
chapters.summary: Evaluate research design and statistics, integrate evidence with context, and conduct decision-focused program evaluation with implementation and equity data.
objectives: Distinguish experimental, quasi-experimental, single-case, and mixed methods.
Interpret validity threats, effect size, and uncertainty.
Design useful program and implementation evaluations.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Design answers a claim
sections.content: Randomized experiments strengthen causal inference through random assignment but still require valid measures, fidelity, attrition analysis, and ethical implementation. Quasi-experiments use comparison groups, interrupted time series, matching, or other structures without random assignment and must address selection. Single-case designs use repeated measurement and replicated phase changes across time, behaviors, people, or settings. Descriptive and qualitative designs can answer prevalence, experience, process, and context questions without pretending to prove causality.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Validity threats and statistical interpretation
sections.content: History, maturation, testing, instrumentation, regression, selection, attrition, and contamination offer alternative explanations for change. Statistical significance reflects evidence under a model, not effect magnitude, importance, or replication. Examine effect size, confidence interval, baseline risk, measure quality, practical benefit, adverse effects, subgroup response, and cost. Correlation does not establish cause; multiple analyses and small samples require additional caution.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Evidence-based practice is an integration
sections.content: Evaluate peer review, design fit, comparison, measurement, implementation, analysis, transparency, replication, population, and setting. Then integrate the best available research with student or system data, professional expertise, stakeholder values, culture, feasibility, and resources. Evidence-based does not mean a brand is universally effective. Define the expected mechanism and outcome, monitor implementation and response locally, and change course when evidence contradicts the plan.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Program evaluation and implementation
sections.content: Decision-focused evaluation states users and decisions, need and theory of change, population and reach, implementation, outcomes, equity, cost or burden, stakeholder experience, limitations, and recommendations. Implementation evidence includes adherence, dosage, quality, responsiveness, adaptations, training, coaching, leadership, and context. Mixed methods can explain how and for whom quantitative outcomes occurred. Protect privacy and report unfavorable findings with the same honesty as favorable ones.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What feature most strengthens causal inference in a randomized experiment?
choices: Random assignment helps balance known and unknown preexisting differences between conditions.
It guarantees perfect implementation.
It eliminates attrition.
It makes measurement validity unnecessary.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Random assignment reduces selection bias, but fidelity, attrition, measurement, ethics, and generalizability still matter.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A school cannot randomize but compares matched schools before and after implementation. What design is this?
choices: A randomized controlled trial.
A quasi-experimental design that can strengthen inference but remains vulnerable to selection and other group differences.
A purely qualitative case study.
A meta-analysis.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Quasi-experiments use comparison or time structures without random assignment and require cautious treatment of alternative explanations.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which single-case feature demonstrates experimental control?
choices: One pretest and one posttest.
A large norm group.
Repeated demonstration that behavior changes systematically when the intervention is introduced, withdrawn when ethical, or staggered across tiers.
A satisfaction survey alone.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Single-case designs rely on repeated measurement and replicated phase relations rather than sample size alone.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Aggression declines over three years without a comparison condition. Which validity threat is especially plausible?
choices: Interrater reliability only.
Face validity only.
Restriction of range only.
Maturation, because normal development over time may account for some change.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Maturation is change due to development or time rather than the intervention and is important in long uncontrolled studies.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A result is statistically significant but has a tiny effect. What should the psychologist conclude?
choices: Examine effect size, confidence interval, practical importance, costs, equity, and study quality rather than equating significance with usefulness.
The intervention is automatically important.
The null hypothesis is certainly false in every setting.
Effect size is irrelevant after significance.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Statistical significance does not establish magnitude, precision, practical value, causal validity, or relevance.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Legal, Ethical, and Professional Practice
chapters.domain: Foundations of School Psychological Service Delivery
chapters.summary: Apply NASP ethical problem solving, IDEA and Section 504 principles, FERPA privacy, consent, competence, reporting, documentation, and current jurisdiction-specific rules.
objectives: Protect informed participation and education records.
Apply evaluation and disability-rights procedures.
Use competence, supervision, and structured ethical decision making.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Consent, assent, privacy, and records
sections.content: Explain services, foreseeable risks and benefits, participation, records, and privacy limits in language the student and caregiver can understand. Follow current requirements for parent consent and student assent or self-determination. FERPA controls access and disclosure of education records; employment in the school does not create unlimited access. Verify legitimate educational interest, consent, and exceptions, disclose only authorized necessary information, use secure systems, and document the basis and recipients.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: IDEA and comprehensive evaluation
sections.content: Before initial evaluation, provide required notice and obtain informed parent consent; consent to evaluate is not consent for initial services. IDEA evaluation uses varied technically sound tools and strategies, includes parent information, addresses all suspected need areas, uses appropriate language and access, and never makes one measure the sole criterion. Teams determine eligibility and educational need. Parents who disagree with an agency evaluation have specific independent educational evaluation rights that must be handled without unnecessary delay.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Section 504, access, and professional competence
sections.content: Section 504 prohibits disability discrimination in covered schools and can require individualized aids and services even without IDEA eligibility. A diagnosis does not automatically prescribe a standard plan. School psychologists contribute evidence within lawful team processes rather than deciding alone. Provide only services for which education, training, supervised experience, consultation, and credentials support competence; obtain supervision or refer when a method, population, or problem exceeds current limits.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Ethical problem solving and protection duties
sections.content: Define the dilemma, stakeholders, rights, power, culture, development, risks, and conflicts. Review current NASP ethics, federal and state law, credential rules, and district policy; consult without unnecessary disclosure; generate options; compare consequences; act, document, and evaluate. For suspected maltreatment, imminent harm, or other protection duties, follow current jurisdictional reporting and crisis procedures promptly, do not promise secrecy, and do not conduct an unauthorized investigation.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent school-psychologist validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Before an initial special-education evaluation, what is generally required?
choices: Provide required notice and obtain informed parent consent for evaluation; consent to evaluate is not consent for initial services.
Assume a teacher referral is consent.
